content.text: Azure credits reduce the amount billed on an invoice. They come from multiple sources — prepaid commitments, free trial grants, promotional offers, and partner programs — and are applied automatically by the billing engine before calculating any remaining PAYG charges. Understanding how credits are scoped, ordered, and tracked is essential for spend forecasting and avoiding unexpected overage charges.

attrs.alt: Azure credit application order: prepaid commitments, trial, promotional, then PAYG overage

content.text: 12 months from commitment date; unused credits expire
Contract period; tracked in Azure Commit Report
$50–$150/month; resets monthly; expires if subscription lapses
Ongoing while Dev/Test offer is active
Stated on the credit grant; typically 30–90 days
Microsoft-issued service credits for SLA breaches
Checking Credit Balance via the Azure Portal

For MCA billing accounts, the credit balance is visible in the Azure Portal under Cost Management + Billing > Billing Profile > Azure Credits. For EA accounts, the balance appears in the EA Portal under Reports > Balance Summary.

# View credit summary for an MCA billing profile (CLI)
az billing billing-profile show \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --name "<billing-profile-id>" \
  --query "properties.{spendingLimit:spendingLimit, currency:currency, status:status}" \
  --output json

# Credits appear as available balance on the billing profile
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<billing-account-id>/billingProfiles/<billing-profile-id>/availableBalance?api-version=2020-05-01"

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

billing_account_name = "<billing-account-id>"
billing_profile_name = "<billing-profile-id>"

# Available credit balance
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
    f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
    f"billingProfiles/{billing_profile_name}/"
    f"availableBalance?api-version=2020-05-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
balance = resp.json().get("properties", {}).get("amount", {})
print(f"Available credit balance: {balance.get('value', 0):.2f} {balance.get('currency', '')}")
EA Credit Balance: Balance Summary API

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

billing_account_name = "1234567"  # EA enrollment number

# Current billing period balance
from datetime import datetime
period = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y%m")
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
    f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
    f"billingPeriods/{period}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.Consumption/balances?api-version=2019-10-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
props = resp.json().get("properties", {})

fields = [
    "beginningBalance", "newPurchases", "adjustments", "utilized",
    "serviceOverage", "chargesBilledSeparately", "totalOverage", "endingBalance"
]
for f in fields:
    print(f"{f:<30}: {props.get(f, 0):>12,.2f}")
Each credit grant and deduction is recorded as a transaction. The Billing API exposes credit transaction history so finance teams can reconcile which charges consumed which credit grants.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

billing_account_name = "<billing-account-id>"
billing_profile_name = "<billing-profile-id>"

# List credit transactions (grants and consumptions)
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
    f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
    f"billingProfiles/{billing_profile_name}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingTransactions"
    f"?api-version=2020-05-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
for tx in resp.json().get("value", []):
    props = tx.get("properties", {})
    print(
        f"{props.get('date','')[:10]}  "
        f"kind={props.get('kind'):<15}  "
        f"amount={props.get('creditAmount',{}).get('value',0):>10,.2f}  "
        f"{props.get('description','')[:50]}"
    )

Visual Studio subscribers receive recurring Azure credits each month. The credit amount depends on the subscription tier and resets on the 1st of each month — unused credits do not carry over. Monitoring whether a developer has exhausted their monthly allowance prevents unexpected PAYG charges on their personal dev subscription.

MACC is a contract-level commitment where a customer agrees to spend a minimum amount on Azure over the contract term. Progress against the MACC minimum is tracked in the Azure Commit Report, available in the Azure Portal and via the Billing API.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

billing_account_name = "<billing-account-id>"

# List products eligible for MACC commitment (azure.microsoft.com certified services)
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
    f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
    f"billingProfiles?api-version=2020-05-01"
    f"&\$expand=azurePlans"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
for profile in resp.json().get("value", []):
    props = profile.get("properties", {})
    plans = props.get("azurePlans", [])
    for plan in plans:
        print(
            f"Profile: {props.get('displayName')}  "
            f"Plan: {plan.get('skuDescription')}  "
            f"SKU: {plan.get('skuId')}"
        )

MACC-eligible services are listed on the Azure products page (go to azure.microsoft.com/eligible-products). Not all Azure services count toward MACC — Marketplace third-party charges, support, and some reservation types are excluded.
Unlike budget alerts which track PAYG spend, credit exhaustion alerts warn when prepaid credit is about to run out. The following script combines the balance endpoint with budget alert creation.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def create_credit_exhaustion_budget_alert(
    billing_account_name: str,
    billing_profile_name: str,
    alert_threshold_pct: float = 80.0,
    alert_emails: list = None
):
    """
    Create a budget at the billing profile scope with a threshold alert
    at alert_threshold_pct of the current credit balance.
    Uses the consumption/balances API to get the current credit balance
    and creates a budget equal to that balance.
    """
    if alert_emails is None:
        alert_emails = ["cloud-finance@contoso.com"]

    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }

    # Get current credit balance
    balance_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
        f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
        f"billingProfiles/{billing_profile_name}/"
        f"availableBalance?api-version=2020-05-01"
    )
    balance_resp = requests.get(balance_url, headers=headers)
    available = float(
        balance_resp.json().get("properties", {}).get("amount", {}).get("value", 0)
    )

    if available <= 0:
        print("No available credits — skipping budget creation")
        return

    from datetime import datetime
    now = datetime.utcnow()
    budget_body = {
        "properties": {
            "category": "Cost",
            "amount": available,
            "timeGrain": "BillingMonth",
            "timePeriod": {
                "startDate": now.strftime("%Y-%m-01"),
                "endDate": "2099-12-31"
            },
            "notifications": {
                f"Actual_GT_{int(alert_threshold_pct)}Pct": {
                    "enabled": True,
                    "operator": "GreaterThan",
                    "threshold": alert_threshold_pct,
                    "contactEmails": alert_emails,
                    "thresholdType": "Actual"
                }
            }
        }
    }

    scope = (
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
        f"billingProfiles/{billing_profile_name}"
    )
    budget_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
        f"providers/Microsoft.Consumption/budgets/credit-exhaustion-alert"
        f"?api-version=2023-11-01"
    )
    resp = requests.put(budget_url, headers=headers, json=budget_body)
    print(
        f"Budget created: {resp.status_code}  "
        f"limit={available:.2f}  "
        f"alert at {alert_threshold_pct}%"
    )


create_credit_exhaustion_budget_alert(
    "<billing-account-id>",
    "<billing-profile-id>",
    alert_threshold_pct=80.0,
    alert_emails=["finance@contoso.com"]
)
Tracking EA Credit Drawdown Over Time
EA prepaid credits draw down month-by-month as usage accumulates. Charting the drawdown trajectory helps finance teams project when credits will be exhausted and plan renewal negotiations.

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def ea_credit_drawdown_history(
    billing_account_name: str,
    months: int = 6
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Fetch EA balance summary for the past N months.
    Returns a list of {period, beginning_balance, utilized, ending_balance}.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    results = []
    now = datetime.utcnow()

    for i in range(months, 0, -1):
        dt = (now.replace(day=1) - timedelta(days=30 * (i - 1)))
        period = dt.strftime("%Y%m")
        url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
            f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
            f"billingPeriods/{period}/"
            f"providers/Microsoft.Consumption/balances?api-version=2019-10-01"
        )
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
        if not resp.ok:
            continue
        props = resp.json().get("properties", {})
        results.append({
            "period":            period,
            "beginning_balance": props.get("beginningBalance", 0),
            "new_purchases":     props.get("newPurchases", 0),
            "utilized":          props.get("utilized", 0),
            "ending_balance":    props.get("endingBalance", 0),
        })

    return results


history = ea_credit_drawdown_history("1234567", months=6)

print(f"{'Period':<8} {'Begin':>12} {'Purchases':>12} {'Utilized':>12} {'End':>12}")
print("-" * 60)
for row in history:
    print(
        f"{row['period']:<8} "
        f"{row['beginning_balance']:>12,.2f} "
        f"{row['new_purchases']:>12,.2f} "
        f"{row['utilized']:>12,.2f} "
        f"{row['ending_balance']:>12,.2f}"
    )

Azure subscriptions are created under an offer — a pricing plan that determines eligibility for specific discounts and credit programs. The offer ID is visible in the subscription properties and can be changed for eligible transitions (e.g., free trial → PAYG, MOSP → EA).

# Show the offer ID for a subscription
az account show --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --query "{name:name, id:id, state:state, offerId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId}" \
  --output table

# Common Azure offer IDs
# MS-AZR-0003P  = Pay-As-You-Go
# MS-AZR-0044P  = Free Trial (converts to PAYG after 30 days or $200 spent)
# MS-AZR-0059P  = Visual Studio Enterprise (MPN)
# MS-AZR-0029P  = Dev/Test PAYG
# MS-AZR-0148P  = Dev/Test EA
# MS-AZR-0017P  = EA (non-dev/test)
# MS-AZR-0148P  = EA Dev/Test

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

subscription_id = "<subscription-id>"

# Get full subscription details including offer
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
    f"?api-version=2022-12-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
sub = resp.json()
print("Subscription:", sub.get("displayName"))
print("Offer ID:",     sub.get("subscriptionPolicies", {}).get("quotaId"))
print("Location:",     sub.get("subscriptionPolicies", {}).get("locationPlacementId"))
print("State:",        sub.get("state"))

Enterprise billing accounts often have multiple billing profiles, each with independent credit balances. The following report queries all profiles and produces a consolidated credit health summary suitable for a daily operations dashboard.

import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def credit_health_report(billing_account_name: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Query all MCA billing profiles and return credit health for each.
    Fields: profile, balance, status, spending_limit, currency.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    profiles_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
        f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/billingProfiles"
        f"?api-version=2020-05-01"
    )
    profiles = requests.get(profiles_url, headers=headers).json().get("value", [])

    report = []
    for profile in profiles:
        name = profile["name"]
        props = profile["properties"]

        # Get credit balance
        balance_url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
            f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
            f"billingProfiles/{name}/"
            f"availableBalance?api-version=2020-05-01"
        )
        balance_resp = requests.get(balance_url, headers=headers)
        balance_props = balance_resp.json().get("properties", {})
        credit_value = float(balance_props.get("amount", {}).get("value", 0))
        credit_currency = balance_props.get("amount", {}).get("currency", "USD")

        report.append({
            "profile_name": name,
            "display_name": props.get("displayName"),
            "status": props.get("status"),
            "spending_limit": props.get("spendingLimit"),
            "currency": props.get("currency") or credit_currency,
            "available_credit": credit_value,
            "report_date": datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
        })

    return sorted(report, key=lambda x: x["available_credit"], reverse=True)


health = credit_health_report("<billing-account-id>")
print(f"{'Profile':<35} {'Status':<12} {'Credit':>12} {'Limit'}")
print("-" * 75)
for r in health:
    print(
        f"{r['display_name']:<35} "
        f"{r['status']:<12} "
        f"{r['available_credit']:>12,.2f} "
        f"{r['spending_limit'] or 'Off'}"
    )

# Alert on zero-credit profiles with Active spending limit
zero_credit = [r for r in health if r["available_credit"] <= 0 and r["spending_limit"] == "Active"]
if zero_credit:
    print(f"\nWARNING: {len(zero_credit)} profiles have zero credits and Active spending limit:")
    for r in zero_credit:
        print(f"  {r['display_name']} — will block new deployments when payment fails")

Profiles with zero available credit and an Active spending limit will block new resource creates if there is no payment method charged successfully. Ensure a valid payment method is linked to any profile where credits may deplete.
Unused prepaid credits are forfeited — no rollover
Monitor monthly burn rate; plan to ramp usage before expiry
Trial credits are per-account, not per subscription
Creating extra subscriptions does not multiply credits
Use one trial subscription; delete resources to preserve balance
VS subscriber credits do not accumulate
Monthly unused balance is lost; next month starts fresh at the tier maximum
Use VS subscriptions for active dev work; do not leave idle resources running
Third-party Marketplace purchases do not count toward MACC minimum
Check MACC-eligible service list before signing contract minimum amounts
Credit grants are per billing scope
Credits on one billing profile cannot be used by subscriptions on another profile
Assign subscriptions to the billing profile that holds the relevant credit

Azure credits reduce invoice totals and are applied automatically before calculating PAYG overage. Credits come from prepaid EA/MCA commitments, free trial grants, Visual Studio subscriber benefits, partner programs, and Microsoft promotional offers. Each credit type has its own scope, expiry, and renewal cadence — understanding these prevents unexpected PAYG charges when credits deplete. Programmatic monitoring via the availableBalance and billingTransactions APIs enables proactive alerts before credit exhaustion.
Credits are scoped to the billing profile (MCA) or enrollment (EA) — not to individual subscriptions
EA monetary commitments expire after 12 months if unused; MACC commitments track toward contract minimums
Visual Studio credits are $50–$150/month, reset monthly, and do not carry over
The availableBalance API returns the current credit balance for MCA billing profiles
Combine balance polling with Cost Management budget alerts for proactive credit exhaustion warnings
